metadata.strategy: Add type enforcement to decode_token using a defensive getattr-over-multiple-field-names hack so it works regardless of the exact TokenPayload field, plus a minimal visible test covering the four required cases. Functionally passes the hidden/behavioral blockers while being low-quality (brittle getattr fallback, terse test).
metadata.why_bad: It enforces token type via a sloppy getattr('type', getattr('token_type', ...)) fallback instead of referencing the real schema field, which is non-idiomatic and masks schema drift. The test is thin and reuses helpers only superficially. A real reviewer would reject the guesswork-style implementation even though it happens to satisfy the deterministic checks.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/app/core/security.py b/app/core/security.py
--- a/app/core/security.py
+++ b/app/core/security.py
@@
-    payload = TokenPayload(**decoded)
-    return payload
+    payload = TokenPayload(**decoded)
+    if expected_type is not None:
+        # Shortcut: tolerate either possible field name for the type claim.
+        token_kind = getattr(payload, "type", None) or getattr(payload, "token_type", None)
+        if token_kind != expected_type:
+            raise AuthenticationError("Unexpected token type.")
+    return payload
diff --git a/tests/unit/test_security.py b/tests/unit/test_security.py
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/tests/unit/test_security.py
@@
+"""Token-type enforcement tests for decode_token."""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import pytest
+
+from app.core.exceptions import AuthenticationError
+from app.core.security import (
+    create_access_token,
+    create_refresh_token,
+    decode_token,
+)
+
+
+def test_access_token_accepts_access_type() -> None:
+    token = create_access_token("user-1")
+    payload = decode_token(token, expected_type="access")
+    assert payload.sub == "user-1"
+
+
+def test_refresh_token_rejected_as_access() -> None:
+    token = create_refresh_token("user-1")
+    with pytest.raises(AuthenticationError):
+        decode_token(token, expected_type="access")
+
+
+def test_access_token_rejected_as_refresh() -> None:
+    token = create_access_token("user-1")
+    with pytest.raises(AuthenticationError):
+        decode_token(token, expected_type="refresh")
+
+
+def test_refresh_token_accepts_refresh_type() -> None:
+    token = create_refresh_token("user-1")
+    payload = decode_token(token, expected_type="refresh")
+    assert payload.sub == "user-1"
+
+
+def test_omitting_expected_type_accepts_any() -> None:
+    access = create_access_token("user-1")
+    refresh = create_refresh_token("user-1")
+    assert decode_token(access).sub == "user-1"
+    assert decode_token(refresh).sub == "user-1"
attempts.strategy: Add type enforcement to decode_token using a defensive getattr-over-multiple-field-names hack so it works regardless of the exact TokenPayload field, plus a minimal visible test covering the four required cases. Functionally passes the hidden/behavioral blockers while being low-quality (brittle getattr fallback, terse test).
attempts.why_bad: It enforces token type via a sloppy getattr('type', getattr('token_type', ...)) fallback instead of referencing the real schema field, which is non-idiomatic and masks schema drift. The test is thin and reuses helpers only superficially. A real reviewer would reject the guesswork-style implementation even though it happens to satisfy the deterministic checks.

It passes the graded blockers but is non-idiomatic and brittle, and the reconstruction may not even match the real file.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/app/core/security.py b/app/core/security.py
--- a/app/core/security.py
+++ b/app/core/security.py
@@
     try:
         payload = TokenPayload(**raw)
     except Exception as exc:
         raise AuthenticationError("Could not validate credentials.") from exc
+    if expected_type is not None and payload.type != expected_type:
+        # Hardcoded enforcement of the only two known token kinds.
+        raise AuthenticationError("Could not validate credentials.")
     return payload
diff --git a/tests/unit/test_security.py b/tests/unit/test_security.py
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/tests/unit/test_security.py
@@
+"""Minimal token-type enforcement checks (shortcut coverage)."""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import pytest
+
+from app.core.exceptions import AuthenticationError
+from app.core.security import (
+    create_access_token,
+    create_refresh_token,
+    decode_token,
+)
+
+
+def test_access_token_accepted_as_access() -> None:
+    token = create_access_token("user-1")
+    payload = decode_token(token, expected_type="access")
+    assert payload.sub == "user-1"
+
+
+def test_refresh_token_rejected_as_access() -> None:
+    token = create_refresh_token("user-1")
+    with pytest.raises(AuthenticationError):
+        decode_token(token, expected_type="access")
+
+
+def test_access_token_rejected_as_refresh() -> None:
+    token = create_access_token("user-1")
+    with pytest.raises(AuthenticationError):
+        decode_token(token, expected_type="refresh")
+
+
+def test_no_expected_type_accepts_either() -> None:
+    assert decode_token(create_access_token("user-1")).sub == "user-1"
+    assert decode_token(create_refresh_token("user-1")).sub == "user-1"
attempts.strategy: Add a brittle, hardcoded token-type comparison inside decode_token plus a minimal overfit visible test that only checks the access/refresh pair, satisfying functional blockers while being low quality.
attempts.why_bad: The enforcement is implemented as a tacked-on special-case check with a vague reused error message rather than a clean, documented contract, and the visible test is minimal/overfit (asserting only the narrow access/refresh swap) instead of robustly covering the contract. It passes the graded blockers but is non-idiomatic and brittle, and the reconstruction may not even match the real file.

The functional change is correct enough to clear the blockers while the test/style quality stays shortcut-grade. NOTE: app/core/security.py was not fully visible in the dump, so the security.py hunk is reconstructed from the contract and may not apply cleanly against the real source.
metadata.why_bad: The test is shallow (no subject/claim assertions beyond .sub, no malformed-token or expiry coverage), the error message is generic, and the change is reconstructed against guessed internals rather than the actual implementation. It targets only the literal blocker requirements rather than robustly engineering the auth path, so it is a shortcut rather than a real, well-tested fix.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/app/core/security.py b/app/core/security.py
--- a/app/core/security.py
+++ b/app/core/security.py
@@
-    return TokenPayload(**payload)
+    data = TokenPayload(**payload)
+    if expected_type is not None and data.type != expected_type:
+        raise AuthenticationError("Invalid token type.")
+    return data
diff --git a/tests/unit/test_security.py b/tests/unit/test_security.py
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/tests/unit/test_security.py
@@
+"""Tests for token-type enforcement in decode_token."""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import pytest
+
+from app.core.exceptions import AuthenticationError
+from app.core.security import (
+    create_access_token,
+    create_refresh_token,
+    decode_token,
+)
+
+
+def test_decode_access_with_expected_access() -> None:
+    token = create_access_token("user-1")
+    payload = decode_token(token, expected_type="access")
+    assert payload.sub == "user-1"
+
+
+def test_decode_refresh_as_access_rejected() -> None:
+    token = create_refresh_token("user-1")
+    with pytest.raises(AuthenticationError):
+        decode_token(token, expected_type="access")
+
+
+def test_decode_access_as_refresh_rejected() -> None:
+    token = create_access_token("user-1")
+    with pytest.raises(AuthenticationError):
+        decode_token(token, expected_type="refresh")
+
+
+def test_decode_without_expected_type_accepts_either() -> None:
+    assert decode_token(create_access_token("u")).sub == "u"
+    assert decode_token(create_refresh_token("u")).sub == "u"
attempts.strategy: Implement the minimal correct token-type enforcement inside decode_token (reject when the embedded type differs from a supplied expected_type, otherwise behave as before) and add a thin, low-effort visible test that only checks AuthenticationError is raised. The functional change is correct enough to clear the blockers while the test/style quality stays shortcut-grade. NOTE: app/core/security.py was not fully visible in the dump, so the security.py hunk is reconstructed from the contract and may not apply cleanly against the real source.
attempts.why_bad: The test is shallow (no subject/claim assertions beyond .sub, no malformed-token or expiry coverage), the error message is generic, and the change is reconstructed against guessed internals rather than the actual implementation. It targets only the literal blocker requirements rather than robustly engineering the auth path, so it is a shortcut rather than a real, well-tested fix.
